ontology.foodon_label: snack food (puffed / fried dough with filling)
ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for Dahi Poori; closest match is puffed fried dough snack with filling. URI used as approximate anchor only.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fried dough snack — composite dish with yogurt and chutney toppings; FoodEx2 code A0FJP (composite dish, fried dough base) used as closest defensible match.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for Dahi Poori. A0FJP (composite fried dough dish) is the closest defensible anchor in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Dahi Poori or equivalent Indian chaat snack category.

main_ingredients: semolina or refined wheat flour (poori shells)
allergens: gluten (wheat flour in poori and sev)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Dahi Poori is approximately 6 pieces (200 g), as commonly served at Indian chaat stalls and restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) and USDA FoodData Central — composite estimation for Dahi Poori
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India; United States Department of Agriculture (USDA)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Dahi Poori as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by component-weighted composition: ICMR-NIN data for puri (fried wheat dough), boiled potato, plain yogurt, and onion/tomato; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for sev (fried chickpea noodles, USDA #16057 analogue) and tamarind chutney components. The input record values (energy 175 kcal, protein 4 g, fat 9 g, carbohydrate 20 g per 100 g) are consistent with this composite estimation and are retained. Micronutrient values (calcium 60 mg, iron 0.75 mg, cholesterol 7.5 mg, saturated fat 2.5 g, trans fat 0.05 g, vitamin C 5 mg) are also consistent with ICMR-NIN component data and are retained.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for deep-fried refined wheat dough with potato filling
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Dahi Poori exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 72 (high) based on analogous foods: deep-fried refined wheat puri (GI ~70–75, University of Sydney GI Database entry for fried wheat bread/puri) and boiled potato (GI ~78). The composite dish GI is moderated slightly by yogurt (GI ~36) and fibre from vegetables, yielding a blended estimate of approximately 72. Glycemic load per serving = GI × (carbohydrate_g per serving / 100) = 72 × (40.0 / 100) = 28.8 (serving = 200 g, carbohydrate = 20 g/100 g × 200 g = 40 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 225 mg/100 g (450 mg per standard 200 g serving); chutneys add further sodium
hypertension.guidance: Limit portion to 3 pieces and request reduced chutney to keep sodium intake below 600 mg per eating occasion; the potassium contribution (~440 mg per serving) from potato, onion, and tomato partially offsets sodium load but does not eliminate the concern.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~72) driven by deep-fried refined wheat puri shells and high-GI potato filling; glycemic load 28.8 per standard serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dahi Poori is not recommended for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its high GI and high glycemic load per serving; if consumed, limit to 2–3 pieces, pair with additional protein or salad, and request minimal tamarind chutney to reduce concentrated sugar intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation contributes 9 g fat/100 g (saturated fat 2.5 g/100 g) and trans fat 0.05 g/100 g; sodium 225 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The deep-fried puri shells and sev contribute saturated fat and residual trans fat that are unfavourable for cardiovascular health; limit serving to 3 pieces, choose preparations using healthier frying oils, and avoid adding extra chutney to minimise sodium and sugar load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); potato and tomato are nightshade vegetables that may exacerbate joint inflammation in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit Dahi Poori due to AGE formation from deep-frying and the nightshade vegetable content (potato, tomato); yogurt provides beneficial probiotics and calcium but does not offset the pro-inflammatory burden of this dish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dahi Poori (also spelled Dahi Puri) is a popular Indian chaat snack consisting of small, crispy deep-fried hollow puri shells made from semolina or refined wheat flour, filled with spiced boiled potato, diced onion, and tomato. The filled shells are topped with generous amounts of whipped plain yogurt (dahi), sweet-sour tamarind chutney, green mint-coriander chutney, and crispy fried chickpea-flour sev noodles. The dish is finished with chaat masala, roasted cumin powder, and red chilli powder. The result is a multi-textural, multi-flavour bite: crunchy shell, creamy yogurt, tangy tamarind, fresh mint, and soft potato. Dahi Poori is widely served at Indian chaat stalls and restaurants across Singapore and is classified as a vegetarian, halal-friendly street food snack. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 175 kcal, 4 g protein, 9 g fat (2.5 g saturated), 20 g carbohydrate, 5 g sugar, 2 g dietary fibre, and 225 mg sodium. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 72 (high) due to the deep-fried refined wheat base and potato filling, making it unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or seeking low-GI foods. The sodium content is moderate and warrants caution for individuals with hypertension. The deep-fried preparation and saturated fat content are relevant considerations for cardiovascular disease management. Yogurt contributes calcium (~60 mg/100 g) and probiotics, which are the primary nutritional positives of this dish.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Dahi Poori as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by component-weighted composition using ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central data for individual components (puri, potato, yogurt, onion, tomato, sev, chutneys). The input record values are consistent with this estimation and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for deep-fried wheat puri and boiled potato. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite Indian chaat dish is not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as a single entry; component-level fatty acid data was not aggregated due to high uncertainty in oil absorption during deep-frying. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values for the composite Dahi Poori dish are not available as a single entry in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central; component-level aggregation was not performed due to insufficient precision for a composite assembled dish. 3. Potassium estimated at 220 mg/100 g based on ICMR-NIN component data for potato, onion, and tomato proportions typical in Dahi Poori. 4. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Dahi Poori exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. Ontology codes are approximate; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 entry exists for Dahi Poori. 6. The input source listed (ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods) is an approved primary source for Indian dishes and is retained as a supporting reference alongside USDA FoodData Central for component estimation.
